cases.prompt: What's the current market price trend for structural steel right now? Check the web.
cases.finalText:
Research plan:
1. Check a few current public sources for structural steel or closely related steel benchmarks.
2. Extract the most recent price direction and any stated month-over-month / year-over-year movement.
3. Synthesize the trend, noting that structural steel pricing can vary by region and product type.

Public web findings:
- Trading Economics’ HRC steel page says HRC steel was **1,192.03 USD/T on June 9, 2026**, **up 5.03% over the past month** and **up 35.00% year over year**; it also says the page was last updated June 10, 2026. URL: https://tradingeconomics.com/commodity/hrc-steel
- Gordian’s April 13, 2026 update says **structural steel cost was $2,519.61/ton in April 2026**, **up 7.50% from Q1 to Q2**, while **down 3.27% year over year**. It also says January 2026 was **$2,343.93/ton**, down 5.38% from the prior quarter. URL: https://www.gordian.com/resources/steel-price-updates
- Procurement Resource’s 2026 steel trend page says **US steel was $1,572/MT in April 2026** versus **$1,294.82/MT in March 2026**, and describes **global steel prices as mixed and range-bound in Q1 2026**, with cost support from inputs/logistics but weak demand and high inventories capping upside. URL: https://www.procurementresource.com/resource-center/steel-price-trends

Current trend summary:
- **Short version:** structural steel appears to be **firming / rising in the near term**, but **still below some year-ago levels** depending on the benchmark.
- The clearest recent signal is **upward momentum in Q2 2026**: Gordian reports a **7.5% quarter-over-quarter increase** in structural steel costs.
- At the same time, the market is **not uniformly in a sustained bull run**: Gordian still shows structural steel **down 3.27% YoY**, and Procurement Resource describes the broader steel market as **mixed and range-bound**, with demand weakness and inventories limiting stronger gains.
- So the best read is: **near-term prices are trending upward, but the market remains volatile and not decisively broken out to the upside across all regions/products**.

Alleato internal evidence:
- None used. This was a public-web-only research question.

If you want, I can also narrow this to **U.S. structural beams**, **rebar**, or **fabricated structural steel** specifically, since those can move differently.

cases.prompt: What's happening in the US commercial construction market this quarter?
cases.finalText:
## Research plan
1. Check Alleato internal context for any relevant project/portfolio signal.
2. Pull current public industry sources on backlog, demand mix, costs, labor, and financing/policy.
3. Synthesize the quarter’s headline themes, separating internal context from public evidence.

## Alleato internal evidence
- No project-specific Alleato context was relevant to this question.
- The only retrieved internal memory was a generic team commitment about Anderson Eye Surgeons budget estimate submission, which does **not** inform the US commercial construction market.

## Public web evidence
- ABC reported its Construction Backlog Indicator rose to **8.8 months in April 2026**, up **0.2 months from March** and **0.1 months from April 2025**. ABC also said its confidence readings for sales, profit margins, and staffing all increased and stayed above 50, indicating expected growth over the next six months. Source: https://www.abc.org/News-Media/News-Releases/abc-contractor-backlog-and-confidence-rise-again-in-april-buoyed-by-data-centers
- ABC said the backlog increase was **highly concentrated among larger contractors**, with data center work driving much of the strength. ABC quoted that **42% of contractors with more than $100 million in revenue** are under contract to work on data centers, versus **7% of smaller contractors**. Source: https://www.abc.org/News-Media/News-Releases/abc-contractor-backlog-and-confidence-rise-again-in-april-buoyed-by-data-centers
- JLL’s 2026 U.S. Construction Perspective says the market is being shaped by **policy volatility, trade-policy uncertainty, immigration enforcement, labor shortages, and cost pressure**, with **deepening divergence by sector and region**. JLL says **data centers, utilities, and infrastructure** remain resilient while other sectors search for footing. Source: https://www.jll.com/en-us/insights/2026-us-construction-perspective
- JLL also says **trade-policy-related material cost increases** are still expected to materialize through 2026, and labor constraints are structural and likely to persist. Source: https://www.jll.com/en-us/insights/2026-us-construction-perspective

## What’s happening in the US commercial construction market this quarter
- The market is **not uniformly weak or strong**; it’s becoming more **two-speed**.
- **Backlog is holding up or improving**, especially for large contractors with exposure to **data centers**.
- **Demand is concentrated** in a few sectors: data centers are the standout, with **utilities/infrastructure** also resilient.
- **Traditional commercial segments** are more uneven and face more caution, driven by policy uncertainty and execution risk.
- **Costs remain pressured** by labor shortages and likely tariff/trade-related increases.
- The practical takeaway this quarter: firms tied to **AI/data-center, power, and infrastructure** work are seeing better momentum, while firms reliant on more traditional CRE work face a tougher, more selective market.

## Bottom line
US commercial construction this quarter looks **stable but highly polarized**: stronger backlog and confidence on the back of data centers and resilient infrastructure work, offset by ongoing labor, cost, and policy headwinds that are squeezing the broader market.

If you want, I can next turn this into a **1-page market brief** or break it down by **office / industrial / retail / healthcare / data center**.

cases.finalText:
Bottom line: Alleato’s mission is to be a true design-build partner from the ground up — not a paper-pushing GC — and to execute projects with ownership, urgency, and discipline. The current strategic push is growth with control: scale revenue, raise standards, hire enough to carry the workload, and build an ownership culture that can support much bigger numbers.

Evidence
- Brandon’s stated positioning is that Alleato is a firm that “build[s] rather than merely manage[s] projects,” paired with the brand line “Your Partner from the Ground Up” in company messaging. That is the clearest mission signal in the source set. [Teams DM, 2026-02-04 / 2026-02-16]
- The company’s strategic horizon from the end-of-year meeting was explicit: reach $1B in revenue by 2032 through employee stock ownership and an ownership culture. [End of the year Meeting/Company event]
- Near-term growth targets from that same meeting were $150M–$200M in 2026 revenue, even after absorbing a $12M project loss. [End of the year Meeting/Company event]
- The company also called for hiring 10–20 new staff to handle workload and improve execution capacity. [End of the year Meeting/Company event]
- The operating emphasis in recent materials keeps pointing to higher standards, internal promotion, accountability, and disciplined execution rather than just chasing volume. [Teams DM, 2026-02-16; Company Quarterly Meeting – April 30th]

What this means
Alleato is not trying to win by being the cheapest or the loudest. The strategy is to build a sharper operating engine: better execution, stronger people, tighter accountability, and enough capacity to scale without turning into chaos. The mission and the goals line up — they both say the company’s edge should be ownership, not just coordination.

Recommended next steps
- Use “build, not manage” and “partner from the ground up” as the default language in client-facing positioning.
- Keep the operating focus on capacity, standards, and accountability; that’s what supports the revenue targets without breaking the machine.
- If you want, I can turn this into a 1-paragraph internal company positioning statement or a tighter executive slide version.

cases.prompt: What's happening in the US commercial construction market this quarter?
failures: expected at least one of [searchConstructionMarket] to fire — none did
observations: tools fired: searchDocuments, semanticSearch, searchMeetingsByTopic, getCompanyKnowledge
cases.finalText:
The quarter reads as **active but tight**. Work is still moving, but the pressure points are financing, permitting, and decision speed — not just raw demand.

What I can support from the system is thinner than I’d like for a national market call. I searched internal docs, semantic results, and meeting intelligence. There’s **no dedicated market report** in the knowledge base, and the company market-intel library came back unavailable. So this is a read based on the signals we do have from current deal and project conversations, not a quoted third-party macro report.

What’s showing up consistently:

- **Financing is gating starts.**  
  In the Union Collective kickoff, the team was waiting on lending options and a term sheet before full mobilization. That’s the pattern: projects aren’t necessarily dead, but they’re getting sequenced around capital certainty. When money gets slower, starts get slower.

- **Permitting and regulatory friction are still real.**  
  Multiple meetings flagged permit delays and jurisdiction-specific issues. Union Collective had fire sprinkler permit rejection risk and a phased-permit discussion just to keep early work moving. That tells you owners and GCs are still trying to break projects into executable pieces because full approvals are taking too long.

- **Preconstruction is more value-engineering-heavy than momentum-heavy.**  
  The PEMB vs. structural steel discussion is a good example: teams are actively comparing systems, lead times, and fee impacts, not just pushing CDs forward. That usually means the market is still price-sensitive and owners want optionality.

- **Lead times are better in some packages, but that’s not the whole story.**  
  One meeting explicitly leaned toward conventional steel because lead times looked favorable relative to complexity. That suggests supply chain panic has eased versus peak disruption years. But improved lead times don’t solve slow decisions, financing drag, or permit bottlenecks.

- **Commercial work is still getting done where there’s a clear business case.**  
  The internal pipeline signals point to continued activity in mixed-use/hospitality-style concepts, warehouse/industrial-related work, tenant improvements, and specialty systems. So this is not a frozen market. It’s a selective market.

What that means in plain English:

- **Industrial / light industrial / warehouse-adjacent work** still looks more resilient than pure speculative office.
- **Adaptive reuse, phased buildouts, and mixed-use concepts** are getting attention because they can be structured around financing and permitting realities.
- **Owners are asking for faster pricing and more scenario analysis** before they commit.
- **Execution discipline matters more this quarter than broad market optimism.** If a team can price quickly, phase permits, and keep lenders confident, work moves. If not, it stalls.

The biggest market risk this quarter:
- **Not cost escalation by itself — uncertainty drag.**  
  Projects lose time in the handoff between concept, funding, permit strategy, and owner decisions. That’s the real tax on the market right now.

The practical takeaway for Alleato:
- Win work by being the team that can **price fast, VE intelligently, break permitting into phases, and show owners a finance-aware path to start**.
- Don’t rely on “market recovery” language. Right now the edge is operational: **certainty beats optimism**.

Confidence / gap:
- Strongest evidence is from live project and pursuit conversations in the system.
- Weakest area is national external benchmark data because I **did not find a current quarter market report** in the knowledge base.

If you want, I can do the next pass two ways:
1. **internal-only market read by sector** based on Alleato’s pipeline and conversations, or  
2. **external market scan** using web research for a true Q2 2026 U.S. commercial construction briefing.
